speaker: interviewer
text: This is Tape 8. The survivor is Mania Lichtenstein. Today is April 1, 1996. Where did you go from the second displaced persons camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people we knew came to-- found-- found out that we are in this camp, and they came to see us and talked us into going to Hamburg with them. They-- they stayed in Hamburg, and they liked it. He said-- they said that life is-- is , quiet. Nobody bothers you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So not having any special requirements in our life of where to go, so we'll go. We'll be with them, because we like this couple very much. They're now in Australia, living.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we went to Hamburg. And I had a little girl already. And it became like, you know, she acclimatized, playing with little German girls, or going to kindergarten. Spoke German only, I must say, because there was no other way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And stayed in Hamburg a few years, until we realized that, hey, now it's time to stop and think about the future. Well, one thing we knew for sure, that staying in Hamburg, or in any place in Europe, would never be, you know? We-- we had to be somewhere, you know, where there is-- not in Europe, not where-- where the soil is soaked with blood. Is this the first time you actually started planning about a future?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first time that you realize that-- how long can you run, place, to place, to place, to place? You-- you have to settle somewhere. Now-- now, I'm a mature woman, I have a baby, you know? You have to think, now, of a home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started-- and then again, it happened to everybody, the-- eventually. After the war, there was chaos, you know? You didn't know, really, where you going or what you are doing. But now, you have to think of more-- you know, stabilize your life. And we started checking out what countries, you know? Again, there were, for displaced persons, camp-- camps that forward you, if you had a relative somewhere, whoever. People went to USA. People went to Canada, to South America, wherever, whoever accepted you. It was good enough. It wasn't our choice. So what was your choice from Hamburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So then, one of our people that we knew left for Canada and, soon enough, started bringing us all over. Of course, you had to guarantee, you had to vouch that the person is a relative of yours that you guarantee, because they didn't want to bring in people and have them as a burden. So she claimed that it was cousins, and so we came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Canada, we came August 5, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part of Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Montreal, Canada. And from then on, normal life began. Because you had to rent an apartment. You had to go get a job. You had to know that you have to pay rent, that you have to go out to a store and buy food, and you need the money to pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So normal life began. It was tough, because you really, you know, you worked for next to nothing. But somehow, after what we'd went through, this was terrific. It was normal. It was-- people lived like this, you know? How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, yeah. Actually, by the Jews, not-- because they didn't understand. Oh, what did you-- like, I don't know. You had such an incident. Hitler didn't kill you? I don't know what it was. Like jealousy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because before long, the-- the-- the refugees-- because you saved every penny. You-- we knew better. We had nothing. We had to start from scratch. So when we had, let's say, $20, you got the week, it was, spend this much on food, this much, this much put away, you know? Because, hey, suddenly, there is a tomorrow. Until now, tomorrow, who knows? But now, suddenly, there is tomorrow, and maybe after tomorrow. Who knows? So people were living so, like, really saving every penny. And before long, all-- all these refugees, in the eyes of those who were not so well-to-do, of course, is hey, how did you manage to live in this apartment? Anyway, this-- this is not an important issue. But-- But did people ask you about your experiences? Did they want to hear?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you want to tell them?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- we didn't talk, until now. My husband passed away in Montreal at age 37, and I still don't-- I did not find out exactly where he was, when, because there was, at that time yet, no talking. Nothing happened. It's a closed book. And I-- I can't understand it. One day, if I find a psychiatrist, I'll sit down and-- and ask him how he can explain it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do in Montreal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well then, in Montreal, life became, you know, kind of normal. But my husband, at age 31, they tell me he has leukemia. I'm pregnant with the second child, and life ends again. And to me, really, it ended, because, what-- and I knew nothing. I cannot go up-- like other women went to factories. I know nothing. I'm useless. And with two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law helped. I mean, without him, I would have never survived. But finally, after one year, I had one neighbor, especially, who was so interested. Go to business college. Go to-- I'd say, how can I go? My head is spinning. I-- I'm just crying. I'm crying day in and day out. Because for four years, six years, he was sick. And my brother-in-law was the only one who knew about it. [INAUDIBLE] And you could not, you would not tell anybody, not even the closest of friends. It's a mentality that you don't-- secret.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, it was a terrible time for me, one of the most terrible. I knew only four years. And-- and you can't cry even. I want to cry, I want to scream now. No, you have to put on a smile, because he should not find out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had two children now, at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two. Yeah. I mean, that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your second child's--

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- then, Brenda was born, in October 5, 1956.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to school? To business school?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- and-- and my husband was still alive. She was three-and-a-half when he died. [SIGHS] And when the-- and when he died, I just was so-- I tell you, maybe I can put in another funny kind of comment. I only wanted to be 60. I thought, if I'd be 60, I'd just lie down and die. And everything is fine and dandy.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is how my mind worked. Only 60. I counted. How many years? How many years? Just 60. I don't know why I chose 60. But here I passed way past 60, and I'm still kicking. So my husband dies. I have to do something with me, even if it's for the-- OK, you need money. And-- and-- and for the reason to comb your hair, to put on a dress, to go out and-- and be with people. And some-- one neighbor, whose response was, go, go. You'll know. You'll know. And don't forget, I was a greenhorn, you know? I was-- and my English was-- but I did read a lot. And I always work. I always have dictionaries, all over. At every corner, you'll find-- because I'm curious. I have to see. If I don't know, I have to find out. And I go to the business college, taking bookkeeping, and typing, and office correspondence. Anyway, who's going to hire me? I'm in my mid-30s. And those days, you hired a 16-year-old after high school, so she was filing. You can't do it now. Now, you have to know everything. So-- but then, the place my husband worked, they found out that I'm looking for a job. And they called me. You see, I-- I had a lot of luck. In my misfortune, I was very lucky, you know? Because, how? How I, of all people, that found ways out?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- they called me. And anyway, this is how I became a bookkeeper. I changed jobs later, and I was the head bookkeeper of a very big, multimillion-dollar enterprise. And I was a bookkeeper all my life, in Montreal. Then, the Quebec political situation, with the upheaval, the bickering. My kids, like most professional people, left the province. And I remained alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: My younger daughter went to the University of Santa Barbara, so she, too, was gone. And everybody started pushing, why don't you go? Why don't you join them? What are you doing here? I picked up, and I came to Scottsdale. What made you pick Scottsdale? Just briefly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because my kids settled in Scottsdale, so I followed. And it was good. They all had-- had all the grandchildren, so you know you had to see them grow up, and being away, all alone and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you continue doing some work, in Scottsdale?

speaker: interviewee
text: As soon as I came. I came on a Friday, Monday, I went to work. My son-in-law had a big enterprise, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What day and month was that, when you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came here, February 28, 1985. Immediately, my son-in-law, he said he couldn't wait for me to come and take over the books, because I'm a good worker, I must-- this, I must say about myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you feel you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fate. Fate. You could have hidden under the table here, and-- and-- and they-- they just overlooked, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What dreams do you have, still?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. [SIGHS] I have the dreams. Longings. You know. [SIGHS] Sisters is, to me, a very precious topic, an item. I think sisters makes you feel so important. You're just somebody, you know? Oh, meet my sister. I-- I-- I can't say anybody, meet my-- who? So [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you often think of your Shoah experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. For many years, it was simply, like, push the button. You knew. You were aware it happened. But oh, no, no. First of all, you were young. You had babies. You had a life to struggle with. There was no time to sit down and think of me, and my experiences, and what I feel, and what I think. You were too busy with--

speaker: interviewee
text: And over-protective. Like, I almost drove them crazy, you know? Because I had to guide them, day in and day out. The babies. Like, I wouldn't let the baby out. What if she falls? Can you imagine? What if she falls? What if she scratches her knee? Her-- So-- How did the experiences affect the way that you raised your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They are very nice children-- normal, definitely normal, if not more. But they admitted to me, you know, you don't talk-- we don't talk a lot, like just, let's talk, let's spill it all out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they did tell me that-- don't think that we are just like anybody else, we are the second generation. And we feel differently. Because after all, they were little, they used to ask me, where are my cousins? Why don't I have cousins? Why don't I have aunts? Why don't-- I wouldn't tell them why, because, well, what can I say? What advice would you have for future generations about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know it's only words. Because when you live a life of almost luxury, I would call it, a life normal with everything you please, every dream you can pursue, you just take it for granted. To experience what I experienced makes you appreciate small, maybe insignificant things, that normal-- like all the normal people, who did not go through-- that money and this. OK. It's good. You have. So you'll buy another dress, or you'll eat another meal, you can't afford it, right? [LAUGHS] But family, treasure, treasure. Family, it is so, so important. And to me, I see my kids-- thank God, they are well established. I mean, thank God.

speaker: interviewee
text: But if I see them living in harmony and respecting one another-- because you could live with less money, too. I know, it's-- it's-- the-- you know, so you have a-- one dress less, or one-- something less. As long as you have a roof over your head and you have to eat-- and thank God, here, in this country, or maybe all over, you-- you eat too much, maybe, you know? So-- so I would say, appreciate. Is there anything else you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: War should never happen again. I hate war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything--

speaker: interviewee
text: War is terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: --else you would like to say or add?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I just hope my kids, or any Jew, or any race in this world, never has to experience cruelty. It-- it bothers me, even when I read a newspaper about people I don't know. I-- I-- I don't even place them anywhere. But why do people do unto others such cruel things?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm terribly meek. I-- I'll-- I'll catch a fly. It could be winter in Montreal, I can't kill it. I take it, throw it out in the cold. It will freeze anyway, but I can't kill it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you, very much, for this most important interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. My duty. I can't say my pleasure, but it's my duty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mania, we took a short break, because you mentioned that there was something important that you wrote. And perhaps you'd like to read that to us now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Memories that won't go away. In the still of the night, sleep does not come. There is pain in my hand and pounding of heart. I see, I hear, I feel their pain, back then, when the world seemed clearly insane. In 1942, from the clear, autumn sky, God looked down on us, not blinking an eye. They were caught like stray dogs and loaded on trucks. Mothers, fathers, little babies and scared, tiny tots, wishing to know what their big crime was. They well could tell where the road led to, to the place called [NON-ENGLISH], they [INAUDIBLE] knew. The place where mass graves stood empty and big, just a few weeks earlier, the Jews were ordered to dig. Mothers looking at children with apologizing eyes. Sorry, my little ones, [INAUDIBLE] their sighs. The one-way journeys soon came to an end, facing the beast, man-made craters, knowing well what they meant. Hurry up, they roared. Jump in, you pigs. 19,000 this time must fill these digs. Lucky for those quickly shot and dead. The others drowned slowly in the blood-filled bed. Mission accomplished. Program number one, great success. And two months later, number two will progress. 6,000 only, a task easy next time. With perfect planning, will take place right on time. Same tracks, same route. Destination well-known. To the thought of death, accustomed they've grown. Their fate was sealed. No chance to redress. Program number two, yet another success. One thousand were left, but for only one year. To produce for the Germans, their much-needed gear. After one year and one month, to be precise, the thousand's fate was no surprise. One town reached perfection-- perfection [GERMAN]-- the Nazis were gloating with genuine pride. And this took place in my hometown, Wlodzimierz. From Polish towns, like mine, forewarn, the Jews were by type and forever gone. It was painful for me to write this piece, but I felt I had placed the [INAUDIBLE]. Easy sleep will never come, for I can't forget and overcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mania Lichtenstein, a survivor. Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother and my father. And this picture was taken, I believe, in 1913. And it was either before or after their wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wlodzimierz, my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found a cousin. I didn't know even of her existence, before. And she-- actually, she found me. And she started sending me pictures that she possessed, that I-- I didn't have any after I survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my-- my oldest sister, Rifka, and my little brother, who passed away at age five. He was born-- he was the first-born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately, when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't know exactly, but he must have been four years old, and she two, or approximately this age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my hometown, Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same way, from that cousin, who had these pictures from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother when she was very young, before she was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately, when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early 1900s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also, in my hometown, Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the same source, my cousin, who had these pictures from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my whole family. My father-- from left, my father. Standing, my oldest sister, Rifka. And next to my father is Nechama. And next to her is-- was I-- is me and my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must have been two years old, my sister four, and the oldest-- 12 or 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in my hometown, Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you obtain this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the same cousin, who had them from before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Left, my grandmother, Heine Feige, and her sister, Mindel [? Prince. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately, when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Hm. I don't know, but way, way back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and her brother, Manes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my hometown, Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's brother, my Uncle Manes. On the other side is his wife, Paje, and the two children, Rosa and Chaim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately, when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Oh, probably 1932, '34, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother. At the other end, her brother Manes, and his wife, Paje.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Wlodzimierz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the family of my older daughter's. In the red dress, it's Jeane, my daughter, her husband, Alan, and the three granddaughters-- from right, Joanna, at the other end, Adena, and sitting next to her mother, Corinne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Scottsdale, Arizona.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger daughter's family. At left, my daughter, Brenda, her husband, Ken. In the middle, Emily, and above her, Alyssa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in San Diego, where they lived at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Jeane, my older daughter, my son-in-law, Alan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken on a cruise, in 1995.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my younger daughter, Brenda, and my son-in-law, Ken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In San Diego.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband, Joseph, and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Hamburg, after the war. I believe, in 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's an important piece of an authentic document from the ghetto, from the last ghetto, where only 1,000 hand-workers were allowed to live. And how I obtained it, I referred to in the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it a copy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, it's a copy. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is the original?

speaker: interviewee
text: At Yad Vashem.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're looking at the same document, and there's some writing in red on the bottom. Can you please tell us what that is and what it means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I just wrote it over, just in case I forget, that infamous Commissar of our district who was signing these kind of documents. This is only a copy. The original was left at Yad Vashem, in Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krauze, Herr Krauze.
